params.label: Background
params.notes: This opens the Background section. Everything ahead sets up the central mystery: how could years of just watching women and a single coin-flip trial reach opposite conclusions about the very same hormones? For now keep the audience curious, not yet informed. Transition: start with the twenty-year detective story.

params.code:
cw = (body_w - 0.5) / 2
ct = body_top + 1.0
ch = 2.55
cards = [
    ("THE EXCITING CLUE  (1996-2002)",
     "Studies that simply WATCHED women found that estrogen users developed Alzheimer's less often, and later, than women who never took it [1, 2]. For years this looked like solid proof that the pills protected the aging brain.",
     TURQUOISE_RGB, _rgb('#0C7A6A')),
    ("THE PLOT TWIST  (2003)",
     "Then the WHIMS trial let a COIN FLIP -- not the woman herself -- decide who got hormones. The memory benefit vanished, and dementia risk actually ROSE in women aged 65 and older [3, 4].",
     DEEPPINK_RGB, DEEPPINK_RGB),
]
for i, (lab, bod, stripe, lc) in enumerate(cards):
    x = body_l + i * (cw + 0.5)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=ct, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=PAPER_RGB)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=ct, width=cw, height=0.09, fill_rgb=stripe)
    _add_text(slide, lab, left=x + 0.28, top=ct + 0.24, width=cw - 0.56, height=0.4,
              size=15, color_rgb=lc, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
    _add_text(slide, bod, left=x + 0.28, top=ct + 0.84, width=cw - 0.56, height=ch - 0.98,
              size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
by = ct + ch + 0.4
_add_rect(slide, left=body_l, top=by, width=body_w, height=0.75, fill_rgb=AMBER_RGB)
_add_text(slide, "Same drug, opposite answer. The rest of this talk is one question:  HOW?",
          left=body_l + 0.3, top=by, width=body_w - 0.6, height=0.75,
          size=19, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True, anchor=MSO_ANCHOR.MIDDLE)
params.notes: Open like a mystery, because it was one. For twenty years, observational studies -- just watching women, not assigning anything -- kept finding that estrogen users had sharper memory and less Alzheimer's [1, 2]. Doctors nearly started prescribing hormones for the brain. Then in 2003 the WHIMS randomized trial flipped a coin to decide who got hormones, and the benefit didn't just vanish, it reversed: dementia risk actually rose [3, 4]. Same drug, opposite answer. Set up the whole talk as: how can two honest studies disagree this badly? Transition: here it is in one picture.
params.title: A twenty-year detective story

params.code:
_fit_image(slide, "/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g6_estrogen/slides/figures/intro_effect_flip.png",
           left=body_l, top=body_top, max_w=body_w, max_h=body_h - 0.85)
_add_text(slide, "Just watching (turquoise) said hormones PROTECT; the coin-flip trial (pink) said they HARM -- the study design, not the drug, decided the answer.",
          left=body_l + 1.0, top=body_bottom - 0.6, width=body_w - 2.0, height=0.6,
          size=14, color_rgb=MUTED_RGB, font=SANS_FONT, italic=True, align=PP_ALIGN.CENTER)
params.notes: This is our most important slide -- read it slowly. Left bar, 'just watching': hormone users looked protected, risk about two-thirds of non-users. Right bar, the coin-flip trial: risk more than doubled. The amber arrow is the sign flip. The dashed line is 'no effect.' The point for a 15-year-old: the STUDY DESIGN, not the drug, decided the answer. Watching can lie; a coin flip cannot. Transition: so why does watching lie here?
params.title: When they flipped a coin, the benefit vanished

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.60
_fit_image(slide, "/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g6_estrogen/slides/figures/intro_confounding_triangle.png", left=body_l, top=body_top+0.10, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h-0.2)
px = body_l + fw + 0.35
pw = body_w - fw - 0.35
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=body_h-0.15, fill_rgb=PAPER_RGB)
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=0.10, fill_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB)
rows = [
  ("Who chose the pill?", "Users were richer, more active, and saw doctors more often to begin with [5]."),
  ("The hidden factor", "That same head start drives BOTH the drug use AND the good memory."),
  ("So the bottom link", "only LOOKS like cause. It is a mirage -- confounding, a.k.a. healthy-user bias [6]."),
]
y = 0.36
for lab, bod in rows:
    _add_text(slide, lab, left=px+0.25, top=body_top+y, width=pw-0.5, height=0.32,
              size=15, color_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
    _add_text(slide, bod, left=px+0.25, top=body_top+y+0.36, width=pw-0.5, height=1.0,
              size=13, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
    y += 1.55
params.notes: Now name the villain: confounding, or 'healthy-user bias.' The women who chose hormone therapy were, on average, richer, more active, and saw doctors more [5]. That hidden head start -- the pink box at the top -- pushes down on BOTH boxes: it makes them more likely to take the pill AND more likely to have good memory. So the dashed bottom arrow, 'takes pill -> better memory,' only LOOKS like cause. It's a mirage created by the third factor [6]. This one diagram is the whole statistical idea of the talk. Transition: you already know this trap from summer.
params.title: Healthy-user bias: a hidden third factor

params.code:
_add_text(slide, "On hot days, ice-cream sales and shark attacks BOTH rise together. Does ice cream summon sharks?",
          left=body_l, top=body_top + 0.1, width=body_w, height=0.85,
          size=18, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
cw = (body_w - 0.5) / 2
ct = body_top + 1.05
ch = 2.5
cards = [
    ("THE ANALOGY",
     "No -- the hot SUMMER WEATHER drives both at once: heat sells more ice cream AND sends more people into the ocean to swim. Neither one causes the other.",
     BLUEVIOLET_RGB, BLUEVIOLET_RGB),
    ("OUR VERSION",
     "Estrogen use and good memory also rise together. But being HEALTHIER and better-off to start with can drive both -- so the pill itself may summon nothing.",
     TURQUOISE_RGB, _rgb('#0C7A6A')),
]
for i, (lab, bod, stripe, lc) in enumerate(cards):
    x = body_l + i * (cw + 0.5)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=ct, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=PAPER_RGB)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=ct, width=cw, height=0.09, fill_rgb=stripe)
    _add_text(slide, lab, left=x + 0.28, top=ct + 0.24, width=cw - 0.56, height=0.4,
              size=15, color_rgb=lc, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
    _add_text(slide, bod, left=x + 0.28, top=ct + 0.84, width=cw - 0.56, height=ch - 0.98,
              size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
ty = ct + ch + 0.42
_add_text(slide, "When two things move together, hunt for the hidden third factor before you believe one caused the other.",
          left=body_l, top=ty, width=body_w, height=0.7,
          size=17, color_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True, anchor=MSO_ANCHOR.MIDDLE)
params.notes: Make it click with the classic. Ice-cream sales and shark attacks rise together every summer -- but ice cream doesn't summon sharks; hot weather drives both. Map it: estrogen use is the ice cream, good memory is the shark attacks, and 'being healthier to start with' is the summer heat. The takeaway line is the transferable habit: when two things move together, hunt for the hidden third factor before you believe one caused the other. Transition: now to our own toy data.
params.title: Ice cream and shark attacks

params.label: Methods
params.notes: This opens the Methods section. We move from the big idea -- confounding -- to the concrete: the actual survey data we used and the one honest question a survey can answer. Plain-language framing: here is exactly what we looked at, and what we can fairly ask of it. Transition: meet the dataset.

params.code:
gap = 0.32
cw = (body_w - 3 * gap) / 4
ch = 2.6
ct = body_top + (body_h - ch) / 2
cards = [
    ("739 WOMEN",
     "all aged 60 or older, drawn from NHANES 2013-14 -- a real US government health survey, not data we made up.",
     TURQUOISE_RGB, _rgb('#0C7A6A')),
    ("USED ESTROGEN?",
     "whether she ever took female hormones -- a simple yes or no, self-reported by each woman.",
     DEEPPINK_RGB, DEEPPINK_RGB),
    ("HER BACKGROUND",
     "her age, education, and family income -- the confounders we have to account for.",
     AMBER_RGB, _rgb('#8A6D00')),
    ("MEMORY SCORE",
     "a digit-symbol attention test; higher = sharper. Our model tries to flag the weaker-scoring half.",
     BLUEVIOLET_RGB, BLUEVIOLET_RGB),
]
for i, (lab, bod, stripe, lc) in enumerate(cards):
    x = body_l + i * (cw + gap)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=ct, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=PAPER_RGB)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=ct, width=cw, height=0.09, fill_rgb=stripe)
    _add_text(slide, lab, left=x + 0.22, top=ct + 0.22, width=cw - 0.44, height=0.4,
              size=14, color_rgb=lc, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
    _add_text(slide, bod, left=x + 0.22, top=ct + 0.8, width=cw - 0.44, height=ch - 0.92,
              size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes: Quick tour of the dataset so the results land. 739 women, all 60+, from NHANES -- a real US government health survey, not something we made up. For each woman we know: did she ever use estrogen; her age, education, and income (the possible confounders); and a memory/attention test score, where higher is sharper. Our model tries to predict who lands in the weaker-scoring half. Keep it light -- they don't need every column, just the shape. Transition: what can we actually ask of survey data?
params.title: Our data: NHANES women 60 and older

params.code:
_add_text(slide, "We cannot randomly ASSIGN estrogen in a survey -- so we can never prove cause here. But we can still ask a narrower, honest question:",
          left=body_l, top=body_top, width=body_w, height=0.6,
          size=17, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
qy = body_top + 0.75
_add_rect(slide, left=body_l, top=qy, width=body_w, height=1.1, fill_rgb=PAPER_RGB)
_add_rect(slide, left=body_l, top=qy, width=0.14, height=1.1, fill_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB)
_add_text(slide, "Does the estrogen effect SURVIVE once we account for age, education, and income?",
          left=body_l + 0.45, top=qy, width=body_w - 0.75, height=1.1,
          size=22, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True, anchor=MSO_ANCHOR.MIDDLE)
cw = (body_w - 0.5) / 2
cy = qy + 1.35
ch = 1.75
cards = [
    ("CRUDE",
     "Estrogen's effect measured all by itself -- ignoring everything else about the woman.",
     DEEPPINK_RGB, DEEPPINK_RGB),
    ("ADJUSTED",
     "Estrogen's effect AFTER we account for her age, education, and income.",
     TURQUOISE_RGB, _rgb('#0C7A6A')),
]
for i, (lab, bod, stripe, lc) in enumerate(cards):
    x = body_l + i * (cw + 0.5)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=cy, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=PAPER_RGB)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=cy, width=cw, height=0.09, fill_rgb=stripe)
    _add_text(slide, lab, left=x + 0.28, top=cy + 0.2, width=cw - 0.56, height=0.4,
              size=15, color_rgb=lc, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
    _add_text(slide, bod, left=x + 0.28, top=cy + 0.74, width=cw - 0.56, height=ch - 0.87,
              size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
fy = cy + ch + 0.22
_add_text(slide, "If the effect SHRINKS from crude to adjusted, the exciting headline was mostly confounding -- not real cause.",
          left=body_l, top=fy, width=body_w, height=0.55,
          size=14, color_rgb=MUTED_RGB, font=SANS_FONT, italic=True)
params.notes: Set expectations honestly. We can't randomly assign estrogen in a survey -- so we can't prove cause. But we CAN ask the narrower question in the box: does the estrogen effect survive once we account for age, education, and income? We'll measure estrogen's effect two ways -- crude (alone) and adjusted (after the confounders). If it shrinks, the crude headline was mostly confounding. That crude-vs-adjusted move is the entire method. Transition: start with the naive, tempting look.
params.title: The one question we can actually ask

params.label: Results
params.notes: This opens the Results section, the payoff. Three slides ahead: the tempting raw headline, the honest adjusted result that shrinks it, and a fairness check on the model. Signal that this is where the numbers finally land. Transition: start with the headline anyone would compute first.

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.56
_fit_image(slide, "/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g6_estrogen/slides/figures/raw_means.png", left=body_l, top=body_top+0.10, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h-0.2)
px = body_l + fw + 0.4
pw = body_w - fw - 0.4
_add_text(slide, "+8.1", left=px, top=body_top+0.3, width=pw, height=1.2,
          size=66, color_rgb=TURQUOISE_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, "points -- the raw gap, users over non-users", left=px, top=body_top+1.55,
          width=pw, height=0.5, size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, "51.8 versus 43.7. Taken at face value, the exciting headline writes itself: 'estrogen users think better.' But we never checked whether the two groups were the same kind of people to begin with.",
          left=px, top=body_top+2.2, width=pw, height=2.2, size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes: The tempting headline, exactly as anyone would first compute it. Average the memory score for users versus non-users: 51.8 versus 43.7, an 8-point head start for the estrogen group. If you stopped here, you'd write 'estrogen users think better' -- the same clue the 1990s studies chased. The whole point of the next slide is that we did NOT stop here. Transition: now adjust for who these women were.
params.title: The tempting headline

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.56
_fit_image(slide, "/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g6_estrogen/slides/figures/confounding.png", left=body_l, top=body_top+0.10, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h-0.2)
px = body_l + fw + 0.4
pw = body_w - fw - 0.4
_add_text(slide, "67%", left=px, top=body_top+0.3, width=pw, height=1.3,
          size=76, color_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, "of the effect was confounding", left=px, top=body_top+1.65,
          width=pw, height=0.5, size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, "The crude effect (-0.33) shrank to just -0.11 once we adjusted for age, education, and income. That missing two-thirds was never the estrogen -- it was that users were healthier and better-off to start with.",
          left=px, top=body_top+2.3, width=pw, height=2.2, size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes: The punchline slide. Same data, honest test. Crude effect: about -0.33 (negative = looks protective). Adjusted for age, education, and income: it collapses to -0.11. That's a 67% shrink -- two-thirds of the 'benefit' was never the estrogen, it was that users were healthier and better-off to begin with. This is confounding caught in the act, a miniature of exactly why WHIMS overturned the observational headlines. If they remember one number from this talk, make it 67%. Transition: one more check any people-model needs.
params.title: Two-thirds of the effect was confounding

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.56
_fit_image(slide, "/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g6_estrogen/slides/figures/fairness_by_group.png", left=body_l, top=body_top+0.10, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h-0.2)
px = body_l + fw + 0.4
pw = body_w - fw - 0.4
rows = [
  ("0.73", "test accuracy for estrogen-USERS", TURQUOISE_RGB),
  ("0.65", "test accuracy for NON-users", MUTED_RGB),
]
y = 0.2
for num, lab, col in rows:
    _add_text(slide, num, left=px, top=body_top+y, width=pw, height=0.75,
              size=44, color_rgb=col, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
    _add_text(slide, lab, left=px, top=body_top+y+0.8, width=pw, height=0.4,
              size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
    y += 1.5
_add_text(slide, "A tool that is reliably right for one group and shakier for another can quietly do harm if a clinic trusts it equally. Naming the gap out loud is the honest move.",
          left=px, top=body_top+y+0.1, width=pw, height=1.6, size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes: A fairness audit, the habit from earlier this week. We trained one model to predict low cognition, then split its accuracy by group: 0.73 for estrogen-users, 0.65 for non-users. A model can look fine overall yet work unequally across groups -- and a clinic that trusts it equally could quietly do more harm to the group it's worse at. The mature move isn't to hide the gap; it's to name it. Transition: and there's something no score in this dataset can see at all.
params.title: Does the model work for everyone?

params.label: What the numbers can’t capture
params.notes: This opens the final section, the honest limits. We step outside the numbers: a test score can say 'fine' while a woman lives with real daily brain fog, and a survey can only hint at cause, never prove it. Framing: good science names what it cannot do. Transition: the tests say fine; she says not fine.

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.58
_fit_image(slide, "/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g6_estrogen/slides/figures/intro_objective_vs_subjective.png", left=body_l, top=body_top+0.10, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h-0.2)
px = body_l + fw + 0.4
pw = body_w - fw - 0.4
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=body_h-0.15, fill_rgb=PAPER_RGB)
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=0.10, fill_rgb=BLUEVIOLET_RGB)
rows = [
  ("The mismatch", "Many women report real, daily brain fog even when the objective test looks perfectly normal [7]."),
  ("Why it matters", "Women's symptoms are too often waved off as stress or emotion instead of investigated [8]."),
  ("Hold both ideas", "The confounding is real AND the patient's account is real evidence -- not noise to dismiss."),
]
y = 0.36
for lab, bod in rows:
    _add_text(slide, lab, left=px+0.25, top=body_top+y, width=pw-0.5, height=0.32,
              size=15, color_rgb=BLUEVIOLET_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
    _add_text(slide, bod, left=px+0.25, top=body_top+y+0.36, width=pw-0.5, height=1.05,
              size=13, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
    y += 1.55
params.notes: Step outside the numbers. Our data is a test SCORE. But many women in menopause report real, daily 'brain fog' even when their objective test looks normal [7] -- the slope lines cross exactly there. And women's symptoms get waved off as stress far too often [8]. The grown-up data-scientist stance holds two true things at once: the confounding is real (the pill may not be the hero), AND the patient's lived report is real evidence, not noise. Transition: so what can this project honestly claim?
params.title: The tests say “fine”; she says “not fine”

params.code:
gap = 0.4
cw = (body_w - 2 * gap) / 3
ch = 2.7
ct = body_top + (body_h - ch) / 2
cards = [
    ("WHAT IT CAN DO",
     "Show that a tempting headline is mostly a mirage, and that adjusting for background shrinks the apparent estrogen effect by about 67%.",
     TURQUOISE_RGB, _rgb('#0C7A6A')),
    ("WHAT IT CANNOT DO",
     "Prove cause. We can only correct for the confounders we actually measured and thought of -- something unrecorded could still be doing the work.",
     DEEPPINK_RGB, DEEPPINK_RGB),
    ("WHAT SETTLES IT",
     "A randomized trial: a coin flip, not the patient, assigns the drug, so the two groups start out identical. That is why WHIMS could overturn 20 years of headlines.",
     AMBER_RGB, _rgb('#8A6D00')),
]
for i, (lab, bod, stripe, lc) in enumerate(cards):
    x = body_l + i * (cw + gap)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=ct, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=PAPER_RGB)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=ct, width=cw, height=0.09, fill_rgb=stripe)
    _add_text(slide, lab, left=x + 0.26, top=ct + 0.24, width=cw - 0.52, height=0.4,
              size=15, color_rgb=lc, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
    _add_text(slide, bod, left=x + 0.26, top=ct + 0.84, width=cw - 0.52, height=ch - 0.98,
              size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes: Be scrupulously honest about scope -- this is what earns trust. CAN DO: show a tempting headline is mostly a mirage and that adjusting shrinks it ~67%. CANNOT DO: prove cause -- we can only correct for confounders we measured and thought of; something unrecorded could still be doing the work. SETTLES IT: a randomized trial, where a coin flip (not the patient) assigns the drug, so the groups start identical. That's why WHIMS could do what twenty years of watching couldn't. Transition: the sources.
params.title: A survey can hint; only an experiment proves

params.code:
y = 0.15
_add_text(slide, "[1] Tang et al. 1996, Lancet -- observational; estrogen users got Alzheimer's less/later.", left=body_l, top=body_top+y, width=(body_w-0.4)/2, height=1.0, size=13, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
y += 1.15
_add_text(slide, "[2] Zandi et al. 2002, JAMA (Cache County) -- observational; long-term users lower risk.", left=body_l, top=body_top+y, width=(body_w-0.4)/2, height=1.0, size=13, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
y += 1.15
_add_text(slide, "[3] Shumaker et al. 2003, JAMA -- WHIMS RCT; hormones DOUBLED dementia risk in 65+.", left=body_l, top=body_top+y, width=(body_w-0.4)/2, height=1.0, size=13, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
y += 1.15
_add_text(slide, "[4] Rapp et al. 2003, JAMA -- WHIMS RCT; no cognitive benefit, scores trended worse.", left=body_l, top=body_top+y, width=(body_w-0.4)/2, height=1.0, size=13, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
y += 1.15
y = 0.15
_add_text(slide, "[5] Wharton et al. 2009, Maturitas -- healthy-user bias; users start out healthier.", left=body_l+(body_w-0.4)/2+0.4, top=body_top+y, width=(body_w-0.4)/2, height=1.0, size=13, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
y += 1.15
_add_text(slide, "[6] Vandenbroucke 2009, Lancet -- why observational studies and RCTs disagreed.", left=body_l+(body_w-0.4)/2+0.4, top=body_top+y, width=(body_w-0.4)/2, height=1.0, size=13, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
y += 1.15
_add_text(slide, "[7] Maki 2024, Harvard Health -- menopause 'brain fog' vs. normal test scores.", left=body_l+(body_w-0.4)/2+0.4, top=body_top+y, width=(body_w-0.4)/2, height=1.0, size=13, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
y += 1.15
_add_text(slide, "[8] Chen & Shafir 2025, Harvard Health -- the dismissal of women's symptoms.", left=body_l+(body_w-0.4)/2+0.4, top=body_top+y, width=(body_w-0.4)/2, height=1.0, size=13, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
y += 1.15
params.notes: The paper trail, in order of the story. Top-left: the two observational studies that raised the exciting clue [1, 2]. Bottom of the left column into the trial: the WHIMS randomized results that overturned it [3, 4]. Right column: the healthy-user-bias explanation [5, 6] and the brain-fog / symptom-dismissal pieces [7, 8]. All were checked against PubMed or the journal page. Transition: one habit to take with you.
params.title: References

params.code:
base = body_top + 0.75
_add_rect(slide, left=body_l, top=base, width=0.16, height=2.4, fill_rgb=TURQUOISE_RGB)
_add_text(slide, "Suspect the chooser, not the treatment.",
          left=body_l + 0.5, top=base, width=body_w - 0.6, height=1.5,
          size=38, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, "When a group that CHOSE a treatment looks healthier, the head start usually belongs to the person, not the pill -- and only a coin-flip experiment can promote a correlation to a cause.",
          left=body_l + 0.5, top=base + 1.7, width=body_w - 1.0, height=1.2,
          size=18, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
_add_text(slide, "And take the patient's account seriously -- even when the test says 'fine.'",
          left=body_l + 0.5, top=base + 3.05, width=body_w - 1.0, height=0.8,
          size=18, color_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
params.notes: Close on the transferable lesson, not the estrogen specifics. Suspect the chooser, not the treatment: whenever a group that CHOSE something looks healthier, the head start usually belongs to the person, and only a coin-flip experiment can turn a correlation into a cause. And -- because this is medicine, about real women -- take the patient's account seriously even when the test says 'fine.' That pair, statistical humility plus listening, is the whole point of the project. Thank you.
params.title: The one habit to carry out the door
